questions.text: Which 3 clinical features most strongly suggest symptomatic fluid overload after hysteroscopy using 1.5% glycine? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: D) Dry mucous membranes and polyuria

questions.explanation: Headache, confusion, seizures, and marked hyponatremia (Na <120) are classic for glycine fluid overload.

questions.text: Which of the following are appropriate first-line strategies for managing intrauterine hemorrhage during hysteroscopic myomectomy? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Direct electrocautery to visible vessel
B) Hysteroscopic resection of uterine septum

questions.explanation: Electrocautery, balloon tamponade, and IV tranexamic acid are first-line. UAE is reserved for refractory cases.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about perforation during hysteroscopy are correct? Select 3 best answers.

answerOptions.text: A) Blunt perforation without electrosurgery may be observed
B) Fundal perforation always requires laparoscopy
C) Electrosurgical perforation mandates abdominal exploration
D) Hemodynamic instability suggests visceral injury

questions.explanation: Observe selected blunt/fundal perforations; electrosurgical perforations need abdominal evaluation; instability implies visceral/vascular injury.

questions.text: Which of the following clinical features are more consistent with vasovagal syncope than anaphylaxis during office hysteroscopy? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Vasovagal: bradycardia, hypotension, nausea, gradual onset. Anaphylaxis: hives, airway symptoms.
questions.submodule: Module 11.4 – Adverse Reaction During Office Hysteroscopy

questions.text: Which 2 complications are most specifically associated with monopolar electrosurgical systems in hysteroscopy? Select 2 best answers.

answerOptions.text: B) Uterine perforation with mechanical dilation
D) Bladder injury from dilator placement

questions.explanation: Monopolar systems use non-electrolyte media (risk of hyponatremia) and can cause capacitive coupling injuries.

questions.text: Which of the following fluid types is electrolyte-poor and carries the highest risk of hyponatremia when used in hysteroscopy?
questions.explanation: Glycine is hypotonic/electrolyte-poor and highest risk for hyponatremia.

questions.text: What is the most appropriate next step if a patient develops headache and nausea postoperatively after a hysteroscopic myomectomy with a 1000 mL glycine deficit?

answerOptions.text: B) Order a basic metabolic panel and monitor fluid status

questions.explanation: Confirm hyponatremia first; escalate therapy based on severity.

questions.text: In healthy patients, what is the recommended maximum fluid deficit for hypotonic, electrolyte-poor media before terminating hysteroscopy?
questions.explanation: For glycine and other hypotonic media, 1000 mL is the usual cutoff in healthy patients.

questions.text: What is the first step in managing significant bleeding during hysteroscopic myomectomy?
questions.explanation: Rule out perforation before proceeding with hemostatic measures.

questions.text: Which of the following is an appropriate tamponade option for intrauterine bleeding?
questions.explanation: A Foley balloon provides effective intrauterine tamponade.

questions.text: If uterine perforation is ruled out and bleeding is diffuse with no focal source, what is the next best step?
questions.explanation: Balloon tamponade is the next step when bleeding is diffuse and perforation is excluded.

questions.text: A perforation during hysteroscopy using a monopolar resectoscope is most concerning due to:

answerOptions.text: C) Thermal injury to intra-abdominal organs

questions.explanation: Active electrosurgery can thermally injure bowel/bladder if perforation occurs.

questions.text: What is the preferred next step in a stable patient with suspected electrosurgical uterine perforation?
questions.explanation: Laparoscopy evaluates for occult thermal/visceral injury.

questions.text: Which of the following most increases the risk for uterine perforation during hysteroscopy?

answerOptions.text: B) Use of a mechanical morcellator

questions.explanation: Cervical stenosis makes entry difficult and increases perforation risk.

questions.text: What is the most common symptom of vasovagal reaction during office hysteroscopy?
questions.explanation: Lightheadedness with nausea/diaphoresis is typical.
questions.submodule: Module 11.4 – Adverse Reaction During Office Hysteroscopy

questions.text: What is the appropriate next step when a vasovagal patient does not improve after IV fluids and positional changes?
questions.explanation: Persistent bradycardia/hypotension warrants atropine.
questions.submodule: Module 11.4 – Adverse Reaction During Office Hysteroscopy

questions.text: Which of the following is a known risk factor for vasovagal reaction during hysteroscopy?

answerOptions.text: D) Use of CO₂ as distending medium

questions.explanation: CO₂ and rigid scopes increase vasovagal risk; saline/flexible scopes reduce it.
questions.submodule: Module 11.4 – Adverse Reaction During Office Hysteroscopy

questions.text: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to endometrial ablation?

answerOptions.text: A) Heavy menstrual bleeding in a patient desiring permanent sterilization
B) History of endometrial hyperplasia without atypia
C) Normal endometrial sampling in a premenopausal patient

questions.explanation: Premalignant/malignant endometrium is a contraindication to ablation.

questions.text: A 42-year-old underwent endometrial ablation and tubal ligation seven years ago. She now reports new-onset cyclic pelvic pain. Ultrasound reveals a distended left cornua with an endometrial stripe of 14 mm. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: PATSS: cyclic pain after ablation + tubal ligation with cornual hematometra/thickening.

questions.text: Which of the following complications is most closely associated with the use of monopolar energy during hysteroscopic endometrial ablation?

answerOptions.text: B) Thermal injury to bowel via capacitive coupling

questions.explanation: Monopolar systems risk capacitive coupling causing adjacent organ thermal injury.
